coredata.dc:title: Elucidating the structure of carbon nanoparticles by ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Meng, Xiangpeng
Choi, Martin M.F.
Gong, Xiaojuan
Chan, Wan
coredata.dc:description: A fast and accurate ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS) method was developed for the separation and structural elucidation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNP). The CNP was synthesised from microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). By using UPLC separation, the CNP product was well separated into ten fractions within 4.0 min. Based on high-accuracy MS and MS/MS analyses, the CNP species were revealed to display six kinds of chemical formulas, including (C10H20N4O5)n, (C8H12N2O5)n, (C16H22N4O9)n, (C6H8O7)n, (C14H18N2O11)n, and (C14H16N2O10)n. In particular, our study revealed for the first time that the CNP species exist as supramolecular clusters with their individual monomers units linked together through non-covalent bonding forces. These findings clearly indicated the usefulness of UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS in identifying the chemical composition of CNP product. It is anticipated that our proposed methodology can be applied to study the structure-property relationships of CNP, facilitating in the production of CNP with desirable spectral features.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS
Non-covalent supramolecular clusters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH on separation of CNP
Effect of MeOH on separation of CNP
Fluorescence detection of CNP using UPLC-FLD
Mass spectrometric analysis of CNP product

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNP aqueous solution. Inset: photographs of dry CNP product (left) and its aqueous solution taken under daylight (right). (B) PL spectra of CNP recorded for progressively longer λ ex with 10 nm increments from 300 nm to 410 nm. Inset: photograph of CNP aqueous solution taken under UV (365 nm) light.

Effect of pH on separation of CNP (0.2 mg/mL). The mobile phase contains 10 mM NH4Ac at pH 4.0‒6.0 or 0.10% FA in water at pH 2.8. All elutions are isocratic at 0.40 mL/min. The chromatograms are monitored with ESI-Q-TOF MS detection.

Effect of MeOH content on separation of CNP (0.2 mg/mL). The mobile phase contains 0.10% FA in water (pH 2.8) and various volume percentages of MeOH. All elutions are isocratic at 0.40 mL/min. The chromatograms are monitored with ESI-Q-TOF MS detection.
Gradient elution of an aqueous CNP sample solution (0.2 mg/mL). The chromatograms are monitored with ESI-Q-TOF MS detection.
Representative MS spectra of CNP fractions. (A–D) are the MS spectra of fractions 1–3, 5, 7 and 9 in different MS ranges.

head.ce:title: Elucidating the structure of carbon nanoparticles by ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong, SAR, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Environmental Science and Engineering, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Environmental Science and Engineering, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Postdoctoral research fellow at The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology.
Present address: Acadia Divinity College, Acadia University, 15 University Avenue, Wolfville, Nova Scotia, B4P 2R6, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and accurate ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS) method was developed for the separation and structural elucidation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNP). The CNP was synthesised from microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). By using UPLC separation, the CNP product was well separated into ten fractions within 4.0 min. Based on high-accuracy MS and MS/MS analyses, the CNP species were revealed to display six kinds of chemical formulas, including (C10H20N4O5)n, (C8H12N2O5)n, (C16H22N4O9)n, (C6H8O7)n, (C14H18N2O11)n, and (C14H16N2O10)n. In particular, our study revealed for the first time that the CNP species exist as supramolecular clusters with their individual monomers units linked together through non-covalent bonding forces. These findings clearly indicated the usefulness of UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS in identifying the chemical composition of CNP product. It is anticipated that our proposed methodology can be applied to study the structure-property relationships of CNP, facilitating in the production of CNP with desirable spectral features.

A fast and accurate ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS) method was developed for the separation and structural elucidation of the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNP) synthesised from microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). After UPLC separation, the chemical structure and molecular formula of each fractionated CNP species were simultaneously elucidated by high-accuracy MS and MS/MS analyses.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles (CNP), which usually refer to a class of spherical carbonaceous nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, exhibit many excellent optical properties, such as stable photoluminescence (PL), broad excitation wavelengths (λ ex) and tunable emission [1]. In striking contrast to the luminescent semiconductor quantum dots, CNP present the additional benefits of favourable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, easy functionalisation, good water solubility and robust chemical inertness [2–6]. Because of these dramatically superior properties, CNP have received growing interests in a wide variety of promising applications, such as bioimaging [7,8], drug delivery [9–13], therapy [14–18], optoelectronic device [19–22], and nanoprobe [23–27]. The utilisation of CNP in diverse applications requires the knowledge of a wide range of properties, the most important of which is the PL property of CNP. Although the intrinsic mechanism of CNP luminescence has not yet been clearly understood, mounting evidence suggest that the chemical structure of CNP has significant influence on the PL performance of CNP [1,28–30]. Given this information, the characterisation methods to be employed must be capable of accurately analysing the chemical structure of CNP. However, attaining this objective is beset by several challenges arising from both the instrumentation requirements and the unique nature of CNP.

The classical techniques for the structural characterisation of CNP are infrared (IR) spectroscopy [31–35], x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [36–40], and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) [41–46]. These techniques certainly provide useful information about the functionalities of CNP. However, they are incapable of directly discerning the exact chemical structure and molecular formula of CNP. Mass spectrometry (MS) exhibits powerful usefulness in revealing information on the chemical structures of smaller-sized nanomaterials (<5 nm) [47]. The story of the mass spectrometric analysis of CNP is very short due to its recent arrival in the nanoparticle world. Recently, matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionisation time-of-flight MS (MALDI-TOF MS) [48,49] has been employed to determine the chemical structure of CNP. This technique provides useful information on the functionalities of CNP fractions isolated from capillary electrophoretic (CE) [48] or high-performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC) [49] separation, but exhibits lower sensitivity with increasing mass; therefore, the attempts in capturing the signals of larger ions are not easy. Besides, MALDI-TOF MS analysis cannot be directly coupled with CE or HPLC separation. For further characterisation by MALDI-TOF MS, the CNP fractions must be collected after analytical separation.

The electrospray ionisation quadrupole time-of-flight tandem mass spectrometry (ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS), which combines the use of soft ionisation technique and sensitive MS detection, has emerged rapidly as an efficient and powerful analytical tool in investigating the assembly of oligomers as well as aggregates [50,51]. In this work, we employed ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with Q-TOF-MS/MS (UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS) to separate and characterise a CNP product synthesised from the microwave pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA). By using UPLC separation, the complexity of the CNP product was revealed. By utilising high-accuracy MS and MS/MS analyses, two tasks-characterising chemical structures and yielding molecular formulas of CNP species-are fulfilled simultaneously. Our proposed UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS methodology provides valuable insights into the chemical composition of a CNP product. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the characterisation of CNP by UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS, which certainly adds to the increasing database on the chemical characteristics of CNP.

Anhydrous CA and formic acid (98.8%) were purchased from Fisher Scientific (Loughborough, Leics, UK). EDA was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). HPLC-grade methanol (MeOH) was from Tedia (Fairfield, OH, USA). Ammonium acetate (NH4Ac, 98.8%) and glacial acetic acid were from BDH Prolabo (VWR International, Leuven, Belgium). Absolute ethanol (EtOH) was from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Distilled deionised (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). All reagents of analytical reagent grade were used as received without further purification unless noted otherwise.

Fluorescent CNP was prepared according to a published method [52] with slight modifications. In brief, 1.0000 g CA was dissolved in 10.0 mL DDI water and then mixed with 0.3126 g EDA (The mole ratio of EDA to CA is 1:1 which was reported to be the optimal condition to prepare CNP with the highest fluorescent intensity [52].) After vigorous stirring for 1.0 min, the clear transparent solution was transferred into a 50.0 mL beaker flask and heated in a domestic microwave oven (1000 W) for 4.0 min. After reaction, the beaker flask was cooled down naturally. The resultant reddish brown and foamy solid was dissolved in 4.0 mL DDI water. Then 36.0 mL anhydrous ethanol was added and centrifuged at a speed of 6000 rpm for 10.0 min to remove the excess precursors and the resulting small molecules. The deposited CNP product was dissolved in 20.0 mL DDI water and further purified by dialysing against 1.0 L DDI water through a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 500–1000 Da (Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 3 days with stirring and recharging with fresh DDI water every 12 h. Finally, a clear and reddish brown aqueous CNP solution was obtained and lyophilised to obtain a dry CNP product. The aqueous CNP solution showed excellent stability with no sign of precipitation for at least 3 months. For comparison, the CNP sample derived from CA only (O–CNP) was prepared according to the above preparation process.

The separation and structural elucidation of the as-synthesised CNP sample were conducted by using an ACQUITY UPLC system coupled with a Xevo G2 Q-TOF mass spectrometer (Waters Corp, Milford, MA, USA). Chromatography was performed on the ACQUITY UPLC separation system, using a Waters UPLC BEH C18 column (2.1 mm × 100 mm, i.d. stainless steel) packed with 1.7 μm octadecyl (C18) bonded silica with 130 Å pore size (Milford, MA, USA). The CNP sample (0.2 mg/mL) was filtered through 0.45 μm, 13 mm i.d. cellulose acetate syringe filters (Alltech, Deerfield, IL) prior to injection. The volume of each injection was 10 μL. The mobile phase contains 10 mM NH4Ac buffer (pH 4.0–6.0) or 0.10% formic acid (FA) in water (pH 2.8) as eluent A and MeOH as eluent B. The flow rate was 0.40 mL/min and the column temperature was maintained at 40 °C. The isocratic condtions with full aqueous mobile phase containing 10 mM NH4Ac at pH 4.0–6.0 or 0.10% FA in water at pH 2.8 was used to study the effect of pH on separation of CNP. The isocratic condtions with mobile phase containing 0.10% FA in water (pH 2.8) and various volume percentages of MeOH (0–20%) was used to study the effect of MeOH on separation of CNP. The gradient elution program was optimised as follows: 100% A for 1.5 min, rapidly decreased to 98% A from 1.5 min to 2.0 min and maintained at 98% A from 2.0 min to 6.0 min. The gradient elution program was also used to analyse the O–CNP product.

The high-accuracy MS and MS/MS analyses of CNP were performed on a Xevo G2 Q-TOF mass spectrometer equipped with a standard ESI interface under the positive ionisation mode. Unless otherwise stated, the optimised source conditions were set as follows: capillary voltage, 2.5 kV; cone voltage, 12 V; collision energy, 15 eV; desolvation temperature, 300 °C; source temperature, 120 °C; cone gas (N2) flow rate, 1 L/h; and desolvation gas (N2) flow rate, 800 L/h. The MS range was scanned from 100 Da to 4500 Da and centroided during acquisition using an internal reference (lock spray) consisting of a solution of 2 ng/mL leucine enkephalin (m/z 566.2771) infused at a rate of 5 μL/min. Data processing was carried out with MassLynx software (version 4.1; Waters).

Fig. 1 A depicts the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-synthesised CNP sample in water. CNP displays two typical absorption peaks located at 236 nm and 350 nm. The peak at 236 nm is ascribed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp 2 domains, and leads to very low fluorescence intensity [53]. The peak at 350 nm is attributed to the n→π* transition of the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing groups, and results in strong emission [35,54–56]. The PL spectra of CNP under different λ ex are displayed in Fig. 1B. It can be seen that the maximum emission is obtained at about 437 nm with a λ ex of 350 nm. Moreover, the emission wavelengths (λ em) are red-shifted from 431 nm to 469 nm with the λ ex increasing from 300 nm to 410 nm, revealing a distribution of various CNP species in the CNP product [57–59]. Fig. S1 (Supplementary data) displays the TEM image of the as-synthesised CNP sample. The particle size of CNP is determined to be dispersed in a range of 2.1–4.6 nm with an average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.3 nm, as shown by the particle size distribution histogram (Inset in Fig. S1) of CNP.

The chemical composition of the as-synthesised CNP sample was determined by elemental analysis. As depicted in Table S1 (Supplementary data), the weight ratio (%) of C, H, O, and N elements in the as-synthesised CNP sample are 47.84, 5.95, 30.55, and 15.66, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted to characterise the functionality of CNP. As shown by Fig. S2 (Supplementary data), CNP displays a broad absorption peak at 3200–3500 cm−1 assigned to the O–H and N–H stretching of the carboxylic acid and amine groups. The IR spectrum of CNP also displays the absorption peaks attributed to the presence of C–H, CO and amido/amine C–N functionalities in CNP. More interestingly, a distinctive absorption peak at 1648 cm−1 attributed to CC stretching is observed. The presence of CC unsaturated bond has also been observed in other fluorescent CNP [60,61]. These results demonstrated the successful formation of CNP that are functionalised with carboxylic acid, amine and amide groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on separation of CNP
ce:section.ce:para: The chromatographic separation of CNP is much dependent on the pH of the mobile phase. Here, the separation of CNP was examined over the pH range of 2.8–6.0. Fig. 2 displays the isocratic elution of the as-synthesised CNP sample at various pH values. At pH 5.0 and 6.0, the separations of CNP species are unsatisfactory. The peaks tend to cluster together and are co-eluted. Fortunately, when the pH is decreased to 2.8–4.5, the separations of CNP species are substantially improved with all the peaks are well resolved.

Meanwhile, the effect of pH on the ionisation of CNP species must be considered for the subsequent mass spectrometric analysis. As shown by the chromatograms in Fig. 2, the peak intensities of all the CNP species at pH 5.0–6.0 are very low. By contrast, substantially improved peak intensities of the CNP species are observed with pH decreasing from 6.0 to 2.8. The results indicate that lower pH benefits in the improvement of the ionisation of CNP species. As such, 2.8 is chosen as the optimal pH for this work since it provides best separation and ionisation for the CNP species in the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MeOH on separation of CNP
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of MeOH content on the separation of the as-synthesised CNP sample is depicted in Fig. 3 . When the MeOH content is 20% v/v, the separation of CNP is unsatisfactory with only one single peak is eluted. Fortunately, as expected, progressively improved resolution of these overlapping peaks is achieved by lowering the MeOH content. However, even a tiny 1% v/v MeOH content is unable to generate complete separation of the CNP species, and only when a full aqueous mobile phase (0% v/v MeOH content) is employed can the complete separation be realised. The results indicate that decreasing the MeOH content in mobile phase could improve the separation of the as-synthesised CNP sample.

To shorten the retention time of the later eluted CNP species, a gradient elution program that starts with a full aqueous mobile phase and progressively increases the MeOH content was employed. Fig. 4 displays the chromatogram of the as-synthesised CNP sample obtained with optimised ESI-Q-TOF MS detection. Under the optimal solvent program, ten separated peaks are found in the chromatogram of CNP, indicating the formation of various CNP species. The separation was completed within 4.0 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CNP using UPLC-FLD
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the ten separated fractions in Fig. 4 are certainly the fluorescent CNP species, the as-synthesised CNP sample was analysed by UPLC coupled with fluorescence detection (UPLC-FLD). Fig. S3 displays the fluorescence chromatogram of CNP monitored at λ ex/λ em of 340/440 nm. It can be seen that the ten separated CNP species displaying the same chromatographic behaviour in Fig. 4 are observed in the fluorescence chromatogram of CNP (Fig. S3). These observations clearly demonstrate the fluorescence feature of the separated CNP species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass spectrometric analysis of CNP product
ce:section.ce:para: Each fraction of CNP labelled in Fig. 4 was subjected to high accuracy MS analysis. The MS spectra of the ten fractions of the as-synthesised CNP sample are displayed in Fig. 5 and Fig. S4. The charges of the major ion peaks are determined by calculating the m/z differences among their isotopic peaks and are labelled on all the MS. Highly reproducible mass patterns are observed among the successive ion peaks in the MS of each fraction of CNP. Fractions 1–3 contain a major mass difference of 276, 216 and 414, respectively; Fractions 4–6 possess a mass major difference of 192; Fractions 7–8 display a major mass difference of 390; and Fractions 9–10 show a major mass difference of 372. These results indicate that the CNP species exist as supramolecular clusters in the aqueous phase with the above-mentioned mass differences as their corresponding monomers units.

To validate this proposal, attempts were first made to characterise each individual aggregates on all the MS by accurate mass analysis. As indicated by Table S2 (Supplementary data), the measured m/z values of each individual aggregates are in excellent agreement with the theoretical m/z values, suggesting that the above-mentioned mass differences are possible monomers units of the supramolecular clusters in the fractions of CNP. As a further test of our proposal, collision-induced dissociation MS/MS (CID MS/MS) analysis was employed. Fig. S5 (Supplementary data) displays the MS/MS of the tetrameric and hexameric aggregates of fraction 1. It can be seen that the tetrameric aggregate at m/z 553 (z = 2) produces a fragment ion of the protonated monomer at m/z 277 (z = 1) while the hexameric aggregate at m/z 829 (z = 2) reveals that the fragment ions of the pentameric aggregate at m/z 691 (z = 2), tetrameric aggregate at m/z 553 (z = 2) and protonated monomer at m/z 277 (z = 1) are the predominant ions. Remarkably similar MS/MS patterns are also observed in the oligomeric aggregates of fractions 2–10 (Fig. S5). These findings unambiguously demonstrate that the above mentioned mass differences are certainly the monomers units of the supramolecular clusters in the fractions of CNP. Since fractions 4–6 possess the same monomer unit of 192, fractions 7–8 have the same monomer unit of 390, and fractions 9–10 possess the same monomer unit of 372, fractions 5, 7, and 9 as well as fractions 1–3 were chosen for the subsequent studies.

In addition, the results from ESI-MS analysis reveal no sign of the elimination of small molecules (e.g. H2O) from the aggregation of the monomers units, indicating that the individual monomers are linked together through non-covalent bonding forces. Similar noncovalently bonded aggregates of other biomolecules have been observed in previous studies [62–66]. It has been reported that the presence of extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding forces favours to stabilize the supramolecular aggregates of serine octameric formed in aqueous phase [62]. For example, Cooks et al. [62] identified a series of higher-order clusters of serine octameric using ESI-MS analyses, and demonstrated that the clustering formation was ascribed to the strong hydrogen-bonding networks formed between the neighboring carboxylic acid groups, and between the hydroxyl and amino groups using collision-induced dissociation MS/MS in combination with density functional calculations. Although the correlation between the functionality of CNP and non-covalent bonding forces has not been reported, the carboxylic acid, amine and amide groups of CNP are similar to those of amino acids.

The potential of ESI conditions to promote clustering was also investigated by varying the capillary spray voltage (1.0, 2.5, and 3.5 kV), the desolvation temperature (150, 300, and 400 °C), and CNP concentration (0.1, 0.2, and 1.0 mg/mL). Figs. S6‒8 show that no experimental evidence is found for any significant change in the MS pattern of the cluster ions, indicating that ESI has no significant effect on cluster distribution and that the supramolecular clusters in each fraction of CNP are formed in the aqueous phase.

To elucidate the chemical structures of the monomers, the MS/MS spectra of the protonated monomer ions of the CNP species (m/z 277 for fraction 1, m/z 217 for fraction 2, m/z 415 for fraction 3, m/z 391 for fraction 7, and m/z 373 for fraction 9) were acquired and are displayed in Fig. S9. For fraction 5, the dimeric aggregate (m/z 385) was selected for MS/MS analysis given that the intensity of its protonated monomer ion (m/z 193) is not sufficiently high to acquire high-quality MS/MS spectra. Based on the fragmentation information shown in Fig. S9, we propose that the m/z 276 monomer has a similar chemical structure to CA by replacing two carboxylic –OH groups with –NH–CH2–CH2-NH2 groups; the m/z 216 monomer possess a similar chemical structure to CA by replacing one carboxylic –OH group with –NH–CH2–CH2-NH2 group and losing one alcoholic –OH group and one H atom to form an unsaturated CC group; the chemical structure m/z 414 monomer can be viewed as the connection of two m/z 216 monomers by losing one water (H2O) molecule through the amidation reaction between one carboxylic –OH group and one –NH2 group; the m/z 192 monomer possesses the same chemical structure to CA; the m/z 390 monomer can be viewed as the connection of one CA molecule with one m/z 216 monomer by losing one H2O molecule through the amidation reaction between the one carboxylic –OH group in CA and one –NH2 group in m/z 216 monomer; and the m/z 372 monomer is generated from m/z 390 monomer by further losing one alcoholic –OH group and one H atom to form an unsaturated CC group. The chemical structures of the monomer of each fraction of CNP are displayed in Fig. 5 and Fig. S9. The molecular formulas (M1‒6) of the monomer in each fraction are given as follows: C10H20N4O5 (M1 = 276.1434), C8H12N2O5 (M2 = 216.0746), C16H22N4O9 (M3 = 414.1387), C6H8O7 (M4 = 192.0270), C14H18N2O11 (M5 = 390.0911), and C14H16N2O10 (M6 = 372.0805). As demonstrated previously, each CNP species exists as non-covalent supramolecular clusters of their corresponding monomers units. Thereby, the molecular formula of each CNP species are obtained as follows: Fraction 1 (C10H20N4O5)n, Fraction 2 (C8H12N2O5)n, Fraction 3 (C16H22N4O9)n, Fractions 4–6 (C6H8O7)n, Fractions 7–8 (C14H18N2O11)n, and Fractions 9–10 (C14H16N2O10)n.

For comparison, O–CNP derived from CA only was obtained and analysed by UPLC-Q-TOF-MS/MS. The chromatogram of O–CNP are deposited in Fig. S10 (Supplementary data). Under the optimal solvent program, three separated peaks are found in the chromatogram. The MS spectra of each fraction were acquired and are displayed in Fig. S11 (Supplementary data). The charges of the major ion peaks are labelled on all the MS. It can be seen that fractions 1–3 display the same reproducible mass pattern with a major mass difference of 192 among the successive ion peaks in the MS, indicating the formation of supramolecular clusters with C6H8O7 (M4 = 192.0270) as the monomers units. As such, (C6H8O7)n is determined to be the molecular formula of the O–CNP species which are the same as that of Fractions 4–6 of CNP synthesised with CA and EDA. However, it is important to note that the other chemical structures observed in CNP synthesised with CA and EDA are not identified in O–CNP. These observations indicate that EDA plays a significant role in regulating the chemical structure of CNP. Our work could indeed assist in the optimisation of the mole ratio of EDA to CA to synthesise CNP with the desired chemical structure.

We have developed an UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS method to separate and characterise a polydisperse CNP product synthesised from microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA and EDA. The CNP species are determined to exist as supramolecular clusters in the aqueous phase with their individual monomers units linked together through non-covalent bonding forces. The ability of our proposed UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS methodology to elucidate the chemical structure and molecular formula of CNP is of paramount importance, particularly for bioimaging, where the chemical structure of CNP has crucial influences on the PL performance of CNP. It is anticipated that our proposed methodology can be applied to study the effect of mole ratio of EDA to CA on the chemical structure of CNP. With the ability to control the chemical structure of CNP, the CNP products with desirable spectral features specific to each reaction may be obtained. The availability of our proposed methodology UPLC-ESI-Q-TOF-MS/MS is essential to gaining a full understanding of structure-property relationships of CNP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology for a Startup Funding (R9310). We express our sincere thanks to the Provost Office of the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology for providing a Post-Doctoral Fellowship to Qin Hu.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

Instrumentation, TEM image of CNP, Elemental analysis data, IR spectra of CNP, Chromatograms of CNP acquired using different MeOH content in the mobile phase, Fluorescence chromatogram of CNP, MS spectra of fractions 4, 6, 8 and 10 in CNP, High-accuracy mass spectrometric analysis results, MS/MS spectra of the oligomeric aggregates of CNP fractions, MS spectra of CNP fractions using various capillary spray voltage, desolvation temperature, and CNP concentration, MS/MS spectra of the protonated monomers of CNP fractions, Chromatogram of O–CNP monitored with ESI-Q-TOF MS detection, MS spectra of O–CNP fractions. This material can be found in the online version at http://www.sciencedirect.com.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.01.034.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Carbon nanodots synthesis, properties and applications
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Photoresponsive quinoline tethered fluorescent carbon dots for regulated anticancer drug delivery
Magnetic iron oxide–fluorescent carbon dots integrated nanoparticles for dual-modal imaging, near-infrared light-responsive drug carrier and photothermal therapy
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for two-photon photodynamic cancer therapy with a low fs laser power density
A multifunctional ribonuclease A-conjugated carbon dot cluster nanosystem for synchronous cancer imaging and therapy
Graphene quantum dots as autophagy-inducing photodynamic agents
Carbon dots as antioxidants and prooxidants
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
Carbon dot hybrids with oligomeric silsesquioxane: solid-state luminophores with high photoluminescence quantum yield and applicability in white light emitting devices
White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots' electroluminescence
Color-switchable electroluminescence of carbon dot light-emitting diodes
